3: The 'scoffers' who mock the parousia promise ('Where is the promise of his coming? For ever since the fathers fell asleep, all things are continuing as they were from the beginning', 2 Peter 3:3-4) reflect an Epicurean-style skepticism about divine intervention in the regularities of nature: if God were going to act, why hasn't he? The Epicurean doctrine of ataraxia (undisturbed natural regularity, governed by atoms, not divine providence) provided intellectual cover for dismissing apocalyptic expectations. Peter's response: the uniformitarians forget that the past was not uniform — the flood was God's catastrophic intervention in the 'natural order' (2 Peter 3:5-6), and the coming fire will be the next. The argument uses Genesis to rebut Epicurean naturalism.
1: The explicit reference to a second letter connects 2 Peter with 1 Peter — the community addressed is the same diaspora churches of Pontus, Galatia, Cappadocia, Asia, and Bithynia (1 Peter 1:1). 'As reminders to stimulate you to wholesome thinking' (dianoia eilikrinēs): eilikrinēs (sincere, pure, lit. 'judged by sunlight,' the practice of holding pottery to sunlight to detect cracks) describes a mind whose judgment is uncontaminated by the self-interested distortions of the false teachers. The reminder-function of the letter is the testamentary agenda established in 2 Peter 1 (2 Peter 3:12-15): Peter writes to ensure the community can remember his teaching after he is gone.
2: 'Recall the words spoken in the past by the holy prophets and the command given by our Lord and Savior through your apostles' — the two-source authority structure (prophets and apostles) is the standard early Christian hermeneutic (Ephesians 2:20: 'built on the foundation of the apostles and prophets'; Revelation 18:20). By identifying both sources explicitly, Peter positions his letter within the authoritative tradition rather than as a novel teaching. The 'command through your apostles' is the entolē of 2 Peter 2:21 — the delivered sacred command. The appeal to both prophetic Scripture and apostolic witness as a unified testimony against the false teachers parallels Paul's double appeal to Scripture and eyewitness tradition in 1 Corinthians 15:1-11.
4: 'Ever since our ancestors died, everything goes on as it has since the beginning of creation' — the scoffers' argument is a form of uniformitarianism: the regularity of natural processes disproves the possibility of eschatological intervention. The 'ancestors' (pateres) are probably both the OT patriarchs and the first generation of Christians, whose deaths without the Parousia is read as disconfirming evidence. This argument was real in the early church: 1 Thessalonians 4:13-18, 1 Cor 15, and 2 Thess 2 all address anxiety about deaths occurring before the expected return. The scoffers here use the delay as ammunition for rejecting the Parousia entirely.
5: 'By God's word the heavens came into being and the earth was formed out of water and by water' — the creation by word (theou logō) invokes Gen 1 ('God said... and it was so'). 'Out of water and by water' (ex hydatos kai di' hydatos) reflects Genesis 1:2 (the tōhû vābōhû over which the Spirit moved, understood as a watery chaos) and possibly the cosmological tradition of Thales (water as the archē, the primary principle of the cosmos) — though more likely it reflects the Gen 1 reading tradition of Second Temple Judaism. The point is that the same divine word that created the world by ordering primordial water also unraveled that order in the flood (2 Peter 3:5-6) and will again unravel it by fire (2 Peter 3:7).
6: 'By these waters also the world of that time was deluged and destroyed' — the flood as cosmic destruction and re-creation (not merely regional disaster) was a widespread reading in Second Temple Judaism. 1 Enoch 10-11 describes the flood as the purging of the Watcher-pollution from the earth; 4Q252 (Qumran Genesis Pesher) reads the flood as the reversal and renewal of creation. Peter uses the flood as a type of eschatological dissolution: as the primordial waters destroyed the first world-order, the coming fire will dissolve the current world-order in preparation for 'a new heaven and a new earth' (2 Peter 3:13). The flood and the fire are two acts of the same divine creative-destructive word.
7: 'By the same word the present heavens and earth are reserved for fire' — the eschatological destruction of the cosmos by fire (pyrōsis) was a Stoic cosmological doctrine (ekpyrōsis: periodic cosmic conflagration followed by renewal — palingenesia). Peter likely appropriates the Stoic vocabulary his readers knew while redirecting it: the fire is not the cyclic Stoic conflagration but the singular divine judgment-fire of the Day of the Lord (Isaiah 66:15-16; Malachi 4:1; Zephaniah 1:18; 2 Peter 3:8). The Stoic audience would recognize the framework; the content — judgment, not mere natural cycle — is distinctly Jewish-Christian. The 'reservation for fire' (tethēsaurismenoi, treasured up) is a dramatic irony: the scoffers who trust in the world's permanence are unknowingly living in a reserved combustion.
8: 'With the Lord a day is like a thousand years, and a thousand years are like a day' — a near-quotation of Psalms 90:4 ('a thousand years in your sight are like a day that has just gone by, or like a watch in the night'). Ps 90 is Moses's prayer meditating on divine eternity against human transience: YHWH's timescale is incommensurable with human calculation. The Rabbis later used this verse to calculate that a 'day of God' equals a thousand years, deriving a 6,000-year world-week followed by a messianic Sabbath. 2 Peter uses Psalms 90:4 not for chronological calculation but for epistemological humility: human impatience about the Parousia reflects the wrong temporal framework. The Lord is not on human time.
9: 'The Lord is not slow in keeping his promise, as some understand slowness. Instead he is patient, not wanting anyone to perish' — the character of God as patient (makrothymei, long-tempered) and unwilling for any to perish reflects the prophetic portrait of divine compassion that delays judgment for repentance. Ezekiel 18:23 ('Do I take any pleasure in the death of the wicked? Rather, am I not pleased when they turn from their ways and live?') and Ezekiel 33:11 express the same divine will. The delay of the Parousia is reframed as divine mercy, not divine indifference or weakness — the same argument Paul makes in Romans 2:4 ('do you presume on the riches of his kindness and forbearance and patience, not knowing that God's kindness is meant to lead you to repentance?').
10: 'The day of the Lord will come like a thief' — the thief-in-the-night image for the sudden and unexpected arrival of the Day of the Lord appears in Jesus's teaching (Matthew 24:43-44; Luke 12:39-40), Paul (1 Thessalonians 5:2-4), and Revelation (2 Peter 3:3; 2 Peter 16:15). It derives from the OT Day of the Lord tradition (Joel 2:9; Amos 5:18-20; Zephaniah 1:14-16) where YHWH comes in sudden judgment. 'The elements will be destroyed by fire' (stoicheia kausoumena lysontai): stoicheia can mean heavenly bodies (Galatians 4:3, 9; Colossians 2:8) or the basic physical components of matter — both traditions support eschatological dissolution.
11: 'What kind of people ought you to be? You ought to live holy and godly lives' — the standard Jewish-Hellenistic diatribe (dialogical preaching form) moves from cosmological assertion to ethical exhortation via rhetorical question. Stoic and Cynic teachers regularly used the question 'what manner of life?' as a transitional device in their discourses on cosmic themes. Peter uses the Stoic-cosmological language of the letter's argument to arrive at the Jewish-covenantal ethical imperative: the impermanence of the present cosmic order creates urgency for holy living, not (as the false teachers imply) freedom from moral constraint. The coming dissolution validates the ethics of the community, not its abandonment.
12: 'Speed its coming' (speudein tēn parousian) — the idea that human repentance or prayer could hasten the coming of the eschatological age was present in early rabbinic tradition (b. Sanhedrin 97b: 'If Israel repented for one day, the son of David would come'; Elijah's task of hastening the age). The concept also appears in Acts 3:19-21 (Peter's speech: 'Repent... so that times of refreshing may come from the Lord'). Whether 2 Peter means that the community's holiness actively accelerates the eschaton or simply that 'looking forward' with active anticipation rather than passive waiting is the right posture is debated, but the tradition of human moral agency affecting the divine timetable was real in Second Temple Judaism.
13: 'A new heaven and a new earth, where righteousness dwells' — the new creation language comes from Isaiah 65:17 ('See, I will create new heavens and a new earth. The former things will not be remembered, nor will they come to mind') and Isaiah 66:22. In Second Temple Judaism this became the standard expectation of the post-judgment age: 1 Enoch 2 Peter 45:4-5, 4 Ezra 7:75, 2 Bar. 2 Peter 32:6, and Revelation 21:1 all draw on Isaiah's vision. 'Where righteousness dwells' (dikaiosynē katoikei): the personification of righteousness as an inhabitant of the new creation echoes Isaiah 32:16-17 and reflects the apocalyptic tradition of the present age as one where unrighteousness dominates and the new age is characterized by the permanent reign of justice.
14: 'Make every effort to be found spotless, blameless and at peace with him' (aspiloi kai amōmētoi) — the language of 'spotless and blameless' is the vocabulary of sacrificial and priestly purity (Numbers 19:2; Hebrews 9:14: 'how much more will the blood of Christ, who through the eternal Spirit offered himself without blemish to God, purify our conscience'). The community is called to embody the purity of the offering — their lives are a form of priestly presentation to God. 'At peace with him' (en eirēnē) adds the covenant-shalom dimension: the relational wholeness that the covenantal relationship with God entails, the opposite of the hostility that false teaching creates.
15: 'Our Lord's patience means salvation, just as our dear brother Paul also wrote you with the wisdom that God gave him' — the reference to Paul's letters as Scripture in 2 Peter 3:16 is here prefaced by the citation of Paul as an ally on the patience-as-salvation theme. Paul wrote about divine patience in Romans 2:4-5 and 2 Peter 9:22-23 in exactly the terms Peter invokes. The fraternal term 'dear brother' (agapētos adelphos) signals that Peter and Paul stand in the same apostolic tradition, directly countering any suggestion that Petrine and Pauline Christianity were in tension. The historical background of Galatians 2:11-14 (Peter and Paul's Antioch confrontation) makes this reconciling reference theologically loaded.
16: 'His letters contain some things that are hard to understand, which ignorant and unstable people distort, as they do the other Scriptures' (hōs kai tas loipas graphas) — this is the earliest explicit reference to Paul's letters as 'Scriptures' (graphai) on a par with the OT. The canonical implications are enormous: by the time 2 Peter was written, Paul's letters were being read in the same hermeneutical framework as the Hebrew Scriptures and subjected to the same kind of interpretive distortion. The 'hard to understand' (dysnoēta) acknowledges the genuine difficulty of Pauline theology (particularly in Romans, where Paul's nuanced treatment of law and grace was being misread in antinomian directions — cf. Romans 6:1: 'Shall we go on sinning so that grace may increase?').
17: 'Since you have been forewarned, be on your guard so that you may not be carried away by the error of the lawless and fall from your secure position' — the community's 'secure position' (stereōmatos) invokes the image of a military fortification. The false teachers threaten to carry the community away (synapachthentes: carried off as spoil of war). This military-security imagery is consistent with the letter's concern for community cohesion against external threat. The 'forewarned' (proginōskontes) positions the community as having an epistemic advantage: the letter itself is the forewarning, and knowing the pattern of false teaching is the community's defensive resource.
18: 'But grow in the grace and knowledge of our Lord and Savior Jesus Christ' — the closing exhortation returns to the epignōsis theme with which the letter opened (2 Peter 1:2-3). The community's defense against false teaching is not primarily argumentative but formational: growth in the genuine knowledge of Jesus Christ. This echoes the letter's opening claim that divine power operates 'through our knowledge of him who called us' (2 Peter 1:3) — the knowledge that enables godliness and equips the community to resist the corrupting influence of those whose knowledge is false. 'To him be glory both now and forever' closes the letter with a doxology that liturgically reinforces the letter's core claim: Jesus Christ, not the false teachers, is worthy of glory.
